topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy pages felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I traced the familiar contours of my life. Every achievement, every supposed turning point, was meticulously cataloged, yet the narrative felt utterly alien. I stared at the dates, the career milestones, the supposed emotional arcs, and a slow, cold hardening began in my chest. This wasn't a memoir; it was a carefully constructed forgery, and I was the unwitting subject. I pushed the heavy binder across the mahogany desk, the sound echoing too loudly in the silent office. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the word tasting like ash. I picked up the pen, not to sign anything, but just to grip the weight of it, feeling a profound sense of being utterly manipulated, a puppet whose strings had been expertly hidden.

[story 2]

My hands trembled slightly as I read the opening paragraph. It detailed my childhood dreams, the very ones I’d whispered into the dark, rendered with a tone that was all wrong—too sentimental, too flattering. A sharp, involuntary laugh escaped me, quickly stifled by a tight clenching of my jaw. I dropped the manuscript onto the floor. The paper seemed to mock me, the ink mocking the reality I inhabited. I stood up, pacing the length of the room, my movements jerky and agitated. I looked around the sterile space, seeing only the architecture of someone else’s narrative, someone else’s version of me. I felt a deep, nauseating revulsion, a burning sensation behind my eyes as I realized the sheer audacity of the deception.

[story 3]

I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest. The air in the room suddenly felt thin, suffocating. Every carefully curated anecdote, every celebrated triumph, now seemed tainted, cheapened by the realization that it wasn't mine. I let out a slow, deliberate breath, the kind one takes before delivering a calculated strike. I picked up the printed document again, turning the pages with slow, deliberate movements, as if examining a particularly grotesque insect. My gaze swept over the flawless prose, and a distinct tightness formed around my throat. I didn't speak immediately; instead, I simply placed the document down with a decisive, heavy thud, the sound final and absolute. The world seemed suddenly sharper, stripped bare of its comforting illusions.
